_patch_meta.description: Phase 8 — 17 new VCGs for the 12 terms that carried no VCG after sub-group creation. VCG descriptions emerge from verse evidence read in Phase 3. Each VCG is scoped to a characteristic-perspective and a sub-group.
notes: After CC creates these VCGs, a further VCREVISE patch is needed to assign individual P-status verses to specific VCGs within their sub-group (Phase 8 continuation).
G3045 liparos (1 verse, Rev 18:14) does not receive a dedicated VCG — it joins an existing group or is held as a singleton pending Phase 9 review.
sha.a.nan 413-002 verses route to M46-C but already have group_id=1862; no new VCG needed.
Pro 6:31 hon was revised to SA — not included in hon routing.

operations.context_description: Term names wealth as the inner obstacle to the kingdom — the rich person whose material condition has become the competing object of loyalty that blocks entry into God's reign; with difficulty a rich person enters the kingdom, for wealth has displaced trust in God as the inner foundation
operations.authoring_notes: Mat 19:23-24; Mar 10:25; Luk 18:23-25 — the kingdom-obstacle pattern

operations.context_description: Term names the rich person whose wealth produces active moral distortion — indifference to the suffering neighbor, self-satisfied consumption, oppression of the poor, and the weeping of eschatological loss when accumulated wealth is judged; wealth has corrupted the inner person's perception of others and of their own condition before God
operations.authoring_notes: Luk 16:19-22; Luk 6:24; Jam 2:6; Jam 5:1; Rev 3:17; Rev 6:15; Mat 27:57 excluded (SA)

operations.context_description: Term names wealth in its Christological reversal and eschatological revaluation — he who was rich became poor for our sake; the materially poor are rich before God; God himself is rich in mercy; wealth vocabulary deployed in its ordered or inverted form to name the true riches that exceed material abundance
operations.authoring_notes: 2Cor 8:9; Eph 2:4 (God rich in mercy); Jam 2:5 (poor rich in faith); Rev 2:9 (poor but rich)

operations.context_description: Term names poverty as the inner posture of dependence and openness to God — the poor in spirit have nothing to offer God and therefore receive everything; poverty of spirit is the foundational inner disposition of the kingdom, the condition in which the person is constitutionally receptive to divine gift; the poor are those to whom good news is proclaimed as its primary address
operations.authoring_notes: Mat 5:3 (poor in spirit); Luk 4:18; Luk 6:20; Mat 11:5; Luk 7:22

operations.context_description: Term names the poor as the moral test of the inner life of the wealthy — how the person with means treats those without determines the quality of their inner character; preferential treatment of the rich over the poor reveals the distortion of the inner person; the poor are dishonored when wealth is honored
operations.authoring_notes: Jam 2:2-3,5-6; Luk 14:13-21; Joh 12:6 (Judas false concern)

operations.context_description: Term names voluntary or material poverty as the condition from which genuine enrichment of others flows — the widow who gave all gave more than the wealthy; the poor man carried by angels exceeded the rich man; distribute to the poor and receive treasure in heaven; poverty as the ground of kingdom-oriented generosity
operations.authoring_notes: Mar 12:42-43; Luk 21:3; Luk 18:22; Luk 19:8; Luk 6:20; 2Cor 6:10 (shared with ploutizō)

operations.context_description: Term names material riches as the choking and deceiving inner force — riches compete with the word of God for the inner person's receptivity and prove fruitless; riches deceive the person about their true condition; accumulated riches rot and are lost; the deceitfulness of riches is the systematic distortion they introduce into the inner life
operations.authoring_notes: Mat 13:22; Mar 4:19; Luk 8:14 (choking riches); Jam 5:2 (riches rotted); 1Ti 6:17 (uncertain riches)

operations.context_description: Term names riches in their revaluation — the wealth of generosity that flows from poverty; the reproach of Christ counted as greater wealth than Egypt's treasures; riches as the measure of divine abundance generously given; the inner person who rightly assesses what constitutes true wealth
operations.authoring_notes: Heb 11:26 (Moses); 2Cor 8:2 (wealth of generosity from poverty); 1Ti 6:17 (God richly provides)

operations.context_description: Term names the self-directed drive to become rich as the inner-being path to ruin — desire to be rich produces temptation, snare, and harmful desires that plunge the person into destruction; the self-declared prosperity of the Laodicean church conceals actual inner wretchedness; wealth accumulated from Babylon's exploitation is lost in judgment
operations.authoring_notes: 1Ti 6:9; Rev 3:17; Luk 12:21 (negative); 1Cor 4:8 (ironic); Rev 18:3,15,19

operations.context_description: Term names becoming rich as the outcome of right orientation toward God and others — not laying up treasure for oneself but being rich toward God; enriched through Christ's voluntary poverty; rich in good works as the form wealth takes in the kingdom; God bestowing his riches on all who call on him
operations.authoring_notes: Luk 12:21 positive (rich toward God); 2Cor 8:9; 1Ti 6:18; Rev 3:18; Rom 10:12

operations.context_description: Term names the enrichment of others as the inner-being goal of apostolic poverty — as poor yet making many rich; enriched in every way to be generous in every way; received abundance becomes the means of others' enrichment; the through-put pattern in which material poverty generates relational and spiritual wealth for others

Term names the voluntary poverty of Christ as the Christological paradigm of the wealth domain — though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich; voluntary self-impoverishment is the means of others' enrichment; this is the definitive statement of the ordered inner response to the domain of wealth
operations.authoring_notes: 2Cor 8:9 — sole verse; Christological pattern grounding the entire M46-C sub-group

operations.context_description: Term names the abundance of divine provision — God who richly provides all things for enjoyment; the adverbial form of richness applied to the generosity of divine giving, which is the proper ground on which the ordered inner response to the wealth domain rests
operations.authoring_notes: 1Ti 6:17 — sole M46-relevant verse

operations.context_description: Term names material riches as the object of misplaced trust, boasting, and self-accumulation — those who trust in their riches fall; riches are sought insatiably and kept to the owner's hurt; wealth accumulated without the power to enjoy it alienates the person from their own possession; the inner condition of wealth without its proper ground is restlessness, grief, and the loss of what was hoarded

Term names material riches as the fruit of wisdom, humility, and the fear of the Lord — Wisdom carries riches and righteousness in her left hand; the king who chose wisdom received riches as the divine gift that followed right priority; wealth received with the power to enjoy it is the gift of God; the righteous person's house contains wealth alongside enduring righteousness

Term names the rich person whose wealth has produced systematic inner distortion — the rich man is wise in his own eyes while the poor man of understanding sees through him; wealth becomes the imagined stronghold and high wall of the inner person; it produces a rough, dismissive inner posture toward others, structural dominion over the poor, violence, and the restlessness that abundance without contentment creates

Term names the rich person examined by wisdom — the rich and poor stand equally before God who made them both; integrity in poverty surpasses wealth in crookedness as the true measure of the person; wisdom observes that wealth constitutes a moral test of the inner person and that shared creatureliness before God levels the social hierarchy of material status
operations.authoring_notes: Pro 22:2; Pro 28:6; 2Sa 12:4 (moral test of the rich)

operations.context_description: Term names the drive to become rich as the inner path to moral failure and self-deception — hastening to be rich brings punishment; the one who loves pleasure will not become rich; the wicked will not hold their wealth; Ephraim's self-declaration of wealth becomes the ground of false moral self-assessment; corrupt leaders declare themselves blessed by the Lord through the enrichment of exploitation

Term names enrichment as the fruit of diligence and divine blessing — the diligent hand makes rich; the blessing of the Lord makes rich and adds no sorrow with it; the Lord makes poor and makes rich according to his sovereign purposes; wealth from blessing is qualitatively different from wealth from toil and is not accompanied by the inner cost that self-generated accumulation carries

Term names the deliberate refusal to be enriched as an act of integrity and wisdom — Abram refused to take anything from the king of Sodom lest the king claim to have made him rich; wisdom counsels against toiling to acquire wealth and commands the discernment to desist; the inner discipline of not-accumulating is the ordered response to the wealth-seeking drive

Term names material richness as the ambient condition within which the inner person's orientation toward God is revealed — the rich land given to Israel became the setting in which they ate, grew fat, and turned from God; the rich food of Babylon's conquest sustains the conqueror's inner self-satisfaction; and in its eschatological form, divine provision produces rich bread as the sign of restored relationship
operations.authoring_notes: Neh 9:25 (rich land → ate → grew fat → turned from God); Neh 9:35; Hab 1:16; Isa 30:23 (M46-B face)

operations.context_description: Term names the material fatness and robustness of accumulated human power as it stands under divine judgment — the stout warriors of Assyria are struck with wasting sickness by the Lord of hosts; the fat flesh of Jacob's former glory grows lean; the outward markers of material prosperity and physical abundance are reduced by the divine action that exposes the inner hubris they supported
operations.authoring_notes: Isa 10:16 (Assyria's stout warriors); Isa 17:4 (Jacob's fat flesh)
